name: Present Tense Verbs
summary: Conjugate Korean verbs in the polite present tense by adding 아요, 어요, or 해요 to the stem.
explanation:
Every Korean verb in the dictionary ends in 다, such as 가다 (gada, to go) or 먹다 (meokda, to eat). To make the polite present tense, drop the 다 to get the stem, then add an ending based on the stem's last vowel.

If the stem's final vowel is 아 or 오 (a bright vowel), add 아요: 가다 becomes 가요 (gayo). For all other vowels, add 어요: 먹다 becomes 먹어요 (meogeoyo). Verbs ending in 하다 become 해요: 공부하다 becomes 공부해요 (gongbuhaeyo, study).

Vowels often contract when they meet. 가 + 아요 becomes 가요, not 가아요, and 오다 + 아요 becomes 와요 (wayo). The same polite present-tense form covers both 'I go' and 'I am going', so 가요 means both depending on context.
pattern: stem + 아요 (bright vowel) / 어요 (other) / 해요 (하다 verbs)

examples.native: 그 사람은 텔레비전을 봐요. (geu sarameun tellebijeoneul bwayo.)

commonMistakes: Keeping the 다 ending in normal speech. 먹다 is the dictionary form; the polite present is 먹어요.
Adding 아요 to a dark-vowel stem. 먹다 takes 어요 (먹어요), not 아요.
Forgetting to contract vowels. 가 + 아요 is 가요, and 오 + 아요 is 와요, never 가아요 or 오아요.
Treating 하다 verbs like regular ones. 공부하다 becomes 공부해요, an irregular but very common pattern.
Trying to build a separate continuous tense. Korean present already covers 'I eat' and 'I am eating'.
tip: Drop 다, check the last stem vowel, and add 아요 for 아/오 or 어요 for everything else. 하다 verbs always go to 해요.
